title: MVCC and why VACUUM exists
description: Postgres keeps row versions for concurrency; updates and deletes leave dead tuples that VACUUM must reclaim.
body: Use when reasoning about bloat, autovacuum, or 'why is my table huge'. Under MVCC every UPDATE writes a new row version and marks the old one dead rather than overwriting, so readers see a consistent snapshot without blocking writers. Dead tuples are reclaimed by VACUUM; if autovacuum can't keep up or a long transaction/replication slot pins the xmin horizon, dead tuples accumulate as bloat that slows scans and inflates indexes. Do not assume DELETE frees space immediately. Actions: monitor dead tuples, tune per-table autovacuum, and clear stuck long transactions. Validation signals include dead-tuple counts stabilizing after tuning. Risk boundary: transaction-id wraparound from never-vacuumed tables forces an emergency and must be escalated early.
